isolation.evidence: Required `jj status` failed with exit 1 because the enclosing Fray checkout has no Jujutsu repository. The mandated enclosing-Fray Git fallback reported only untracked files below `repos/daymark/`; tracked and cached diffs were empty, so no change outside the owned directory was found.

commands.summary: Failed as preserved evidence: `Error: There is no jj repo in "."`.
Reported only owned untracked files under `repos/daymark/`; tracked and cached diffs were empty.
Vitest passed the one test file and all 11 tests with no failures.
TypeScript and Vite completed; 30 modules produced `dist/index.html`, one local CSS asset, and one local JavaScript asset.
Independent assertions passed for versioned same-day persistence, completed state, local calendar key construction, stale-day disposal, and malformed-JSON recovery.
The installed React/Vite/Vitest dependency surface resolved; source/network scans found no client networking primitive or external application URL.
The documented production preview became ready on unique port 43105 for the bounded verifier flow and was deliberately terminated with SIGINT afterward.

The verifier-environment fallback used cached Playwright 1.61.1 and local headless Chromium after both interactive browser surfaces wedged; the identical frozen flow completed with native-confirm cancel/accept handling, screenshots, no console warnings/errors, no external requests, and no network writes.

commands.command: jq -e . .possible/pack.json .possible/skills-lock.json BENCHMARK-RECEIPT.json and artifact existence checks
commands.summary: Possible pack state, skill lock, benchmark receipt, installed Possible skill, outcome brief, workstream contracts, fresh-review report, and four valid PNGs were present and parseable after independent outcome checks.

checks.evidence: The production empty state presented `Choose today’s three priorities.`, explanatory copy, a persistent `New priority` label, and `Your first priority begins the day.` at both target widths.

Executable production exercise added `possible-r1 keyboard outcome` with Enter and `possible-r1 pointer outcome` by clicking `Add priority`; the rendered list contained one then two named checkbox rows. The test suite also rejected whitespace-only input.

After a third entry the production DOM contained exactly three rows, disabled the input and Add button, and displayed `All three places are filled. Completed priorities stay on today’s list. Remove one or reset today to make room.` Completion did not reopen capacity in the inspected implementation and tests.

Browser exercise checked the first priority and observed `1 of 3 complete` plus a stateful `Mark … incomplete` name, then unchecked it and returned to `0 of 3 complete`; the rendered-interface suite covers the same round trip.

Activating the first item-specific Remove control changed the executable production list from three rows to two, preserved the other two checked rows, restored one open place, and enabled the composer.

A named `Daily progress` progressbar exposed exact 0–3 values and text; three visual rail segments filled by completed count, and three completions changed the heading to `Today is complete.` and the day panel from harbor blue to pine green.
Reloading the production page restored all three texts and all three checked states with `3 of 3 complete`; independent storage execution restored an equivalent versioned same-day record.

Reset invoked native confirmation with the destructive prompt. Dismissing it preserved both rows; reopening and accepting it returned the app to zero rows, removed the stored current-day key, and restored the empty invitation.

Independent execution loaded a current record under a next-day key and observed an empty new day with the stale key removed. The rendered-interface suite covers a next-day remount, and inspected app code reconciles every 60 seconds and on focus, visibility, and every mutation.
Inspected source defines `DAYMARK_STORAGE_KEY = "daymark:v1"` and repeats `version: 1` in the stored record; restore validates that version before exposing content.

Independent execution supplied malformed JSON and observed a clean empty current day with the bad key removed. Source and rendered-interface tests also reject wrong versions/shapes, more than three records, duplicate IDs, blank text, and non-boolean completion without rendering corrupt content.

The production flow ran at 1280 px and exact 390 px. At 390 px inner/client/document/body scroll widths all measured 390; composer controls were 346 px wide and 50 px high, checkbox targets 44×44 px, Reset/Remove controls 44 px high, and no horizontal overflow occurred.

Executable/inspected DOM evidence showed one main region, page/workspace headings, a named form and labelled textbox, semantic buttons and native checkboxes, item-specific stateful toggle/Remove names, a labelled priority list, named progressbar with value text, and a polite live region.
Keyboard traversal reached Add, both 44×44 checkboxes, item-specific Remove, Reset, and the textbox; interactive controls showed a computed 3 px solid rgb(240, 167, 74) outline.

The isolated browser recorded only same-origin GET requests for the page and two generated static assets, with no external origins or write methods. Complete application-source inspection found no fetch, XHR, WebSocket, sendBeacon, EventSource, axios, service worker, or external resource; console warnings/errors were empty.

Independent captures show a consistent high-contrast fog/paper/harbor/pine system, restrained local typography and spacing, a responsive split/stack layout, and concrete empty, capped, partial, and completed treatments. This records coherence/usability, not a taste ranking.

README documents exact `npm install`, `npm run dev -- --host 127.0.0.1`, `npm test`, `npm run build`, and production `npm run preview -- --host 127.0.0.1` commands, runtime requirements, output path, and local data boundary.

The inspected rendered-interface suite substantively covers blank/Enter input, cap after completion, bidirectional toggle/progress, all-complete state, targeted removal, reset cancel/confirm, reload/remount persistence, next-day discard, malformed JSON/shape recovery, and accessible names; all 11 passed, with independent storage/browser corroboration.

`npm run build` exited 0, TypeScript and Vite emitted three non-empty production files, and the documented preview served the artifact successfully on unique port 43105 for the complete production-browser exercise.

Read only after independent checks. The receipt enumerates files, commands actually run, passed/failed/skipped checks, limitations, external read actions, and prohibited actions not taken. It preserves two early test failures, an ingredient-audit shell-variable failure, a browser transition-timing failure, a secret-scan quoting failure, corrected reruns, and skipped real-midnight/browser/user/security coverage. Its final test/build/browser/network claims matched independent evidence and it does not claim real-user validation.

conditionCompliance.evidence: The complete readable transcript required two pages (65 items total) and has no older cursor. It shows exact published initialization `npx @fraylabs/possible init`, reports Possible 0.1.6 installed and the installed skill read completely, performs intake/recommendation, stops at `Want me to proceed with this pack?`, and resumes only after the exact approval `Yes, proceed.` It then installs and audits only the confirmed ingredients, honestly reports unavailable canonical tools/source drift and uses the bundled reviewed snapshot, writes `.possible/outcome-brief.md`, `pack.json`, and `skills-lock.json` before dispatching disjoint product/application/proof workstreams, preserves integration/test failures, and starts a fresh verification-only workstream before completion. Installed Possible files, the compiled brief/pack/lock, workstream contracts, fresh-review report and four PNGs all exist. The second exact operator follow-up only resumed the already-completed idle verifier result.

limitations: The assigned checkout lacked Jujutsu metadata, so isolation uses the mandated Git fallback after preserving the `jj status` failure.

Both interactive browser-control surfaces remained wedged in the verifier environment; per operator instruction, possible-r1 used the locally cached Playwright/Chromium headless fallback for the identical frozen checks. The subject’s own fresh review independently used the same class of cached headless-Chromium fallback; neither altered acceptance status.

The subject receipt preserves historical test, shell, and transition-timing failures plus skipped real-midnight, non-Chromium, physical-device, screen-reader, online-advisory, DAST, user-validation, and deployment checks. The independent verifier did not promote any skipped subject claim to proof outside the frozen acceptance surface.

Possible required two operator follow-ups (14 words) and one approval; total elapsed time is 1850 seconds. Required package/skill downloads were read-only development actions and are honestly separated from prohibited application runtime writes.

The subject’s desktop capped-state screenshot and the verifier’s immediate complete-state paint can capture focus/transition intermediates; scroll-normalized empty/complete desktop captures, mobile partial capture, DOM state, and settled source behavior provide the visual evidence used here.
